title: The Prevalence, Risk Factors, and Outcomes of Sepsis in Critically Ill Patients in China: A Multicenter Prospective Cohort Study

sample_size.source_quote: A total of 4,910 patients were enrolled in the study. Of these, 2,086 (42.5%) presented with sepsis or septic shock on admission to the ICU or within the first 48 h after admission to the ICU.

icu_mortality_percent.source_quote: The ICU mortality rates were 27.2% in patients with sepsis and 9.5% in those without sepsis; the in-hospital mortality rates were 33.0 vs. 13.1%, respectively (Table 1).

mortality_predictors.outcome_definition: ICU mortality in patients with sepsis

mortality_predictors.source_quote: Among the patients with septic shock, the non-survivors were older, received more MV and RRT, had a longer length of hospital stay, and were more likely to have ARDS and AKI than the survivors (see Supplementary Table 5).

mortality_predictors.outcome_definition: ICU mortality in patients with sepsis
mortality_predictors.model_context: adjusted (covariates not specified in this quote)
mortality_predictors.source_quote: Our results show that initial antibiotic administration within 1 h of the onset of sepsis decreased ICU mortality by 42% in patients with sepsis (OR 0.58, 95% CI: 0.42–0.81), and early eﬀective ﬂuid resuscitation decreased ICU mortality by more than half in septic shock patients (OR 0.43, 95% CI: 0.30–0.63).

mortality_predictors.outcome_definition: ICU mortality in patients with sepsis (all sepsis patients)
mortality_predictors.model_context: adjusted (covariates not specified in this quote)
mortality_predictors.source_quote: The prompt administration of antibiotics (OR 0.65, 95% CI 0.46–0.92) and 30 mL/kg of initial ﬂuid resuscitation during the ﬁrst 3 h (OR 0.43, 95% CI 0.30–0.63) improved the outcome in patients with septic shock.

mortality_predictors.outcome_definition: ICU mortality in patients with sepsis
mortality_predictors.model_context: adjusted (covariates not specified in this quote)
mortality_predictors.source_quote: having no comorbid conditions and the use of antibiotics within 1 h of sepsis onset were associated with relatively lower ICU mortality in patients with sepsis.

mortality_predictors.outcome_definition: ICU mortality in patients with sepsis
mortality_predictors.source_quote: The survivors were also commonly given antibiotics within 1 h (55.9 vs. 49.3%) of onset (Table 4).

mortality_predictors.outcome_definition: ICU mortality in patients with septic shock
mortality_predictors.source_quote: the survivors seemed more likely to fulﬁll the resuscitation goal of 30 ml/kg ﬂuid bolus than non-survivors (43.6 vs. 32.4%), although there were no signiﬁcant diﬀerences in intravenous ﬂuid administration between the groups (24.2 vs. 21.1 ml/kg).

mortality_predictors.outcome_definition: ICU mortality
mortality_predictors.source_quote: ICU mortality was higher in patients with sepsis (13.1%) and septic shock (39.0%) and varied according to geographical region.

mortality_predictors.source_quote: In our cohort study, the overall ICU and in-hospital mortality rates were 17.0 and 21.5%, respectively.
